punta-cana.title: Punta Cana
punta-cana.gettingThere: Fly to Punta Cana International Airport (PUJ) — the busiest airport in the Caribbean with direct flights from most US and Canadian cities plus Europe. Taxi or shuttle to Bavaro Beach resorts ~RD$1,500 ($25) in 20 min.

essentials.gettingAround: Most visitors use resort shuttles or organized tours. Taxis between resort areas RD$500-1,500. Uber works in the area. Renting a car useful for Macao Beach and non-resort exploration.
trainConnections: Day trip to Saona Island (boat from Bayahibe)
essentials.language: Spanish, good English at resorts and tourist areas

santo-domingo.title: Santo Domingo
santo-domingo.tagline: The First City of the Americas
santo-domingo.gettingThere: Fly to Las Americas International Airport (SDQ). Taxi or Uber to Zona Colonial ~RD$1,500-2,000 ($25-35) in 30 min. Direct flights from Miami, New York, Madrid, and across the Caribbean.

essentials.gettingAround: Zona Colonial is completely walkable. Metro covers main corridors (RD$20/ride). Uber and DiDi widely available. Taxis use meters or negotiate — agree on price before riding.
essentials.language: Spanish, some English in tourist areas and hotels

samana.title: Samana
samana.gettingThere: Fly to El Catey International Airport (AZS) or drive from Santo Domingo (3 hr). The Samana highway makes the drive scenic and fast. Buses from Santo Domingo ~RD$500.

essentials.gettingAround: Rent a car or use moto-taxis (motoconchos). Boats to Cayo Levantado from the port. Tour operators run whale watching and Los Haitises excursions from the town dock.
essentials.language: Spanish, limited English except at hotels

puerto-plata.title: Puerto Plata
puerto-plata.tagline: Cable Cars, Amber & the Golden Coast
puerto-plata.gettingThere: Fly to Gregorio Luperon International Airport (POP). Taxi to town center ~RD$1,000 ($17) in 15 min. Direct flights from New York, Miami, Toronto, and European charters.
highlights: Teleferico (cable car) to Pico Isabel de Torres

essentials.gettingAround: Motoconchos (motorcycle taxis) are the cheapest way around. Taxis for longer trips. Gua-guas (minibuses) connect towns along the coast. Uber limited — use local taxis.
essentials.language: Spanish, some English in tourist zones

la-romana.title: La Romana
la-romana.gettingThere: Fly to La Romana Airport (LRM) or Punta Cana (PUJ) and drive 1.5 hr. Casa de Campo resort has its own private airport. Buses from Santo Domingo ~RD$400.

essentials.gettingAround: Taxis and motoconchos for local trips. Boats from Bayahibe to Saona and Catalina islands. Casa de Campo has internal golf cart transport. Renting a car useful for independence.
trainConnections: Boat to Saona Island (1 hr from Bayahibe)

cabarete.title: Cabarete
cabarete.gettingThere: Fly to Gregorio Luperon Airport (POP) and take a taxi or shuttle to Cabarete (~RD$1,500, 25 min). Gua-guas from Puerto Plata run frequently (~RD$100).
highlights: El Choco National Park caves & cenotes

essentials.gettingAround: Cabarete is small and walkable along the beach road. Motoconchos for short trips. Gua-guas along the coast. Rent a scooter for Playa Encuentro and El Choco.
essentials.language: Spanish, good English and French in the expat community

jarabacoa.title: Jarabacoa
jarabacoa.tagline: City of Eternal Spring & Adventure Capital
jarabacoa.gettingThere: No airport — drive from Santiago (1.5 hr) or Santo Domingo (2.5 hr). Caribe Tours and other bus companies run from Santo Domingo (~RD$400). The mountain road from Santiago is scenic.
highlights: White water rafting on Rio Yaque

essentials.gettingAround: Motoconchos for local trips. Rent a car for waterfalls and remote trails. Tour operators like Rancho Baiguate arrange adventure activities and Pico Duarte treks from town.

constanza.title: Constanza
constanza.tagline: The Dominican Alps — Cool Mountains & Farmland
constanza.gettingThere: No airport — drive from Santo Domingo (2.5 hr) or Jarabacoa (2 hr). The mountain roads are winding but spectacular. Limited public transport — rental car recommended.

essentials.gettingAround: A car is essential — public transport is very limited. Roads to Valle Nuevo require 4WD. Local guides available for hiking and birding. Bring warm layers — temperatures drop to 5-10C at night.
trainConnections: Drive to San Jose de Ocoa (1.5 hr)

barahona.title: Barahona
barahona.tagline: The Untouched Southwest — Larimar, Eagles & Wild Beaches
barahona.gettingThere: Fly to Maria Montez Airport (BRX) or drive from Santo Domingo (3.5 hr). Limited flights — most visitors drive. The coastal road south of Barahona is one of the DR's most scenic drives.

essentials.gettingAround: Rental car essential for exploring the southwest coast. Boats to Bahia de las Aguilas from Cabo Rojo. Local guides for Lago Enriquillo and Larimar mine. Roads can be rough — SUV recommended.

las-terrenas.title: Las Terrenas
las-terrenas.tagline: The Bohemian Beach Town with French Flair
las-terrenas.gettingThere: Fly to El Catey Airport (AZS, 20 min drive) or drive from Santo Domingo (2.5 hr via Samana highway). Gua-guas from Samana town (~RD$150, 40 min).

essentials.gettingAround: Motoconchos are the cheapest option. Rent a scooter or ATV to explore beaches. Small town — walkable along the main road. Tours to El Limon waterfall by horseback or hiking.
trainConnections: Drive to El Limon waterfall (30 min)
essentials.language: Spanish, good French and some English

travelTips.currency: Dominican Peso (DOP/RD$). Cards accepted at resorts and cities. Always carry cash for small towns, motoconchos, and colmados. ATMs (Banco Popular, BHD Leon, Banreservas) widely available — withdraw in multiples of RD$1,000.
travelTips.visaFree: US, UK, Canadian, and EU citizens can purchase a tourist card (RD$600/$10 USD) for 30 days, extendable to 60. No visa required. Passport must be valid for duration of stay.
travelTips.bestOverallMonths: December to April (dry season). Whale watching in Samana January-March. Hurricane season June-November, though September-October are highest risk.
travelTips.winterNote: The DR has consistent tropical temperatures year-round: coastal areas 27-32C, mountain towns like Jarabacoa and Constanza can drop to 10-18C. Pack layers for the mountains.
travelTips.simCard: Buy a Claro or Altice SIM at the airport for ~RD$500-1,000 ($8-17) with 4G data. Coverage good in cities and tourist areas, spotty in mountains and remote southwest.
travelTips.transport: Caribe Tours and Metro are the main intercity bus companies. Gua-guas (minibuses) cover local routes cheaply. Motoconchos (motorcycle taxis) everywhere. Uber works in Santo Domingo and Punta Cana. Domestic flights limited — most travel is by road.
